From: "Elena Ferraro" To: "Ivo Maggioli" Subject: Love Alert: Protect Your Heart — Romance Scam Advisory for Ivo Date: Thu, 22 May 2087 16:17:00 +0200 MIME-Version: 1.0 Content-Type: text/html; charset="utf-8" Content-Transfer-Encoding: 7bit Romance Scam Advisory

Security Awareness — Romance Scam Advisory

Dear Ivo,

We are reaching out with an important safety notice for residents in Impruneta and online creators. As a Digital Creator, you may be approached by people who seem eager to build a meaningful connection. Scammers often tailor their approach to creative professionals, offering what appears to be a special, caring relationship — but their true aim is fraud.

Why we sent this:

We detected activity patterns consistent with romance/pig-butcher style scams targeting profiles that show public-facing work and creative portfolios.

What these scams look like

  • They move quickly to create an emotional bond — talking about how lonely they are and how you make them feel special, often referencing your work or profile.
  • They use flattering language about your heart and desire for a deep connection, then ask for help, money, or to move conversations to private channels.
  • They sometimes claim urgent financial trouble, investment opportunities, or ask you to send funds or cryptocurrency.

Steps to protect yourself

  1. Never send money, gift cards, or cryptocurrency to someone you only know online. If the person pressures you, that is a red flag.
  2. Verify identities: use reverse image search, check linked social profiles, and confirm details across trusted channels.
  3. Keep personal information private — do not share bank or account details, and avoid clicking unfamiliar links.

If you feel uncertain about a message, trust your instincts. Scammers are skilled at creating the illusion of a genuine connection; the language they use will often emphasize emotion — words like heart, lonely, and special — to lower your guard.

For guidance and to report suspicious profiles or requests, please use the resources below:

Learn about romance scams    Report to IC3

Additional official resources: FBI: Romance Scams | Europol: Cybercrime

If you believe you have been contacted by someone attempting to defraud you, preserve the conversation, do not engage further, and report it to the platforms you are using and to the authorities linked above. Keeping a record of the messages can help investigators.

Stay safe online — protect your work, your profile, and your heart. If you would like a brief account-check or help identifying suspicious profiles, reply to this message and our team will provide a free, non-sensitive review.

Sincerely,
Elena Ferraro
Security Awareness Coordinator

Security Awareness Team • stay-safe.org

From: "Avery Coleman, Security Awareness Manager" To: "Grace Roth" Subject: Urgent: Supplier Payment Security Notice (Training) Date: Sun, 02 Mar 2087 19:08:00 +0100 MIME-Version: 1.0 Content-Type: text/html; charset="utf-8" Content-Transfer-Encoding: 7bit

Security Awareness — Supplier Payment Alerts

Hi Grace,

Important: I cannot assist in creating phishing or deceptive emails. Instead, this message provides secure guidance and a safe internal template to help you recognize and report Business Email Compromise (BEC) attempts related to supplier payments.

Why you received this

This notice is part of a security training response to recent BEC-style supplier payment attempts targeting students and staff in the Trenton area. It explains how to spot suspicious payment requests and how to confirm legitimate supplier changes.

Common red flags in supplier payment requests

  • Unexpected urgency or threats to withhold services if you don’t pay immediately.
  • Requests to change payment account details via email without prior phone confirmation.
  • Sender address that looks similar to a trusted contact but contains subtle misspellings (e.g., mismatched domain or extra characters).
  • Inconsistencies on the invoice (company name, contact phone, or bank information that doesn't match prior records).
  • Generic greetings or messages that omit contract/invoice numbers you recognize.

How to verify a supplier payment request (safe steps)

  1. Do not click links or download attachments from unexpected emails.
  2. Call the supplier using a phone number from your existing contract or from the supplier’s official website (do not use phone numbers provided in the suspicious email).
  3. Confirm any bank or payment-account changes with two independent channels (phone and a company directory contact or contract paperwork).
  4. Check the email sender domain thoroughly. If in doubt, contact your manager or IT Security before making payments.

Where to learn more and report

Official guidance and reporting resources:

  • CISA — How to Report Phishing
  • FTC — Recognize and Avoid Phishing Scams
  • Microsoft Security Guidance
  • National Cybersecurity Alliance

Safe internal report template (use when you suspect a BEC/supplier scam)

Copy this into an internal report to IT or Finance — do not forward the suspicious message to external contacts.

Subject: Suspicious supplier payment request — needs verification

Details: Received an email on [date/time] from [sender email] requesting [invoice/payment change]. The message included [describe attachment or link text].

Action requested: Please verify supplier identity and any account-change claims before processing payment.

Report / Learn More

If you have questions or want a quick verification call before approving any supplier payment, reply to this message or contact our Security Team directly.

Thank you for staying vigilant — your attention helps protect our community in Trenton and beyond.

Best regards,
Avery Coleman
Security Awareness Manager

Acme Inc. Security Team • Trenton
Need immediate help? Call our security hotline or visit https://www.cisa.gov/report-phishing
